sentence: Having successfully developed and field-tested the key components of its network infrastructure, OmniBox, Inc. seeks $7 million in funding to support the national roll out, marketing and sales of the OmniBox Interactive Broadcasting Network in 1997.
The simple reason is demographic: while computers can be found in over 30 percent of U.S. homes (Statistical Research, Inc., 1995), every household that cares to has one TV -- if not more.

## **Figure 1 — Cable TV On-Line Revenue Forecasts by Two Market Research Firms**
Market researchers project a $1 billion market for on-line, interactive services by the year 2000 (see Figure 1).
OmniBox projects $7.08 million in net income from gross sales of $27.78 million in 1997; and $36.1 million net income in 1998; and net income of $110 million in the fifth year of operation (2001).
OmniBox seeks a $7 million equity investment to commence operations of the OmniBox Network in 1997.
A 1996 report by Frost & Sullivan projects the market for interactive television and associated advertising to reach $1 billion by 2002.

A 1995 survey by Statistical Research, Inc. found that 25 percent of today’s households could support an interactive delivery system requiring a co-located TV, phone line and PC, while over 80 percent could use a system requiring only a co-located TV and phone line, and 75 percent could receive interactive TV delivered over a cable network.

_**Small to Mid-Sized Cable Operators**_ Approximately 11,000 cable systems in the small to mid-sized category serve 20 million U.S. cable subscribers; in fact, 90 percent of TCI’s systems have 20,000 subscribers or less.

But while the Time Warner’s with their large subscriber base and the cash-rich telcos can afford to underwrite these services, smaller operators with their limited subscriber base cannot raise the capital to finance new head-end equipment or the $300-and-up set-top boxes that would provide digital interactive services to subscriber homes.

Focusing on these smaller MSU operators in 1997, OmniBox will offer a $175 set-topand-financing package that requires only a $100 capital outlay (what the operator currently pays for analog set-tops) and bank financing for the remaining $75-per-box.

Implementing a typical Full Service Network such as Time Warner’s is estimated to cost $50,000 per video stream for real-time encoding alone, without file servers, sophisticated switching networks, amplifier overhauls and other system costs.
Operators can implement the OmniBox Network for _$10,000 per video_ stream and _no_ head-end costs.
Although untested, this market has potential in the $100 million-and-up market range by the year 2000.
The two key enablers will be $300 (retail) CD-ROM recorders (estimated in 1998) and greater digital bandwidth into the home (1996 and after).
any pay-TV service, as virgin markets for pay-TV services (a $3.4 billion worldwide market in 1994).

_**Lowest cost solution**_ -- For cable operators, the combination of no head-end rewiring, an economical $10,000-per-video stream encoding cost, and set-top financing represents the most affordable solution available.

Among his achievements was a merger agreement with a well-known radio broadcasting entity resulting in net profits of $20 million within 18 months; an $80 million debt restructuring agreement for below-market terms; and sale of an asset at an 80 percent premium over the historical valuation.
Knollmeyer assisted entertainment, broadcasting and media clients in acquisitions of aggregate transaction value exceeding $600 million.

Vandervort increased profitability in a mature market, developing key executive-level relationships among the Multiple Cable System Operator (MSO) companies who control 60 percent of the industry’s retail volume -- and who represent a major market for OmniBox.
Although untested, this market has potential in the $100 million-and-up market range by the year 2000 according to Aberdeen Group.
The two key enablers will be $300 (retail) CD-ROM recorders (estimated in 1998) and greater digital bandwidth into the home (1996 and after).

For an equity investment of $7 million, OmniBox’s complete, ready-to-use system solution will give investors as well as customers early access to the potential multi-billion home-based electronic commerce market, with revenue-generating results that anticipate strongly profitable operations beginning with the first year of operations.
